title: Switch
summary: A toggle lets users switch a setting on or off.
preamble:
A toggle is used for settings with two possible states, where the user can turn something on or off.
The change takes effect immediately, allowing the user to see the result without needing to confirm or save. Both states are clear and equally valid.

overview.title: Behaviour
overview.section-content: Changing the toggle updates the state immediately. The current state should always be clear to the user, and the component should provide feedback for different interaction states such as hover, focus, pressed, and disabled.

columns.content: The toggle is available in two sizes: default and small. The small size should only be used in compact views where space is limited, such as dense layouts or smaller components.

Labels describe the setting, not the state. They should be short and specific, remain clear in both on and off states, and not require additional context to understand.

For example, use labels like “Email notifications”, “Show balance”, or “Enable dark mode”. Avoid labels such as “On / Off”, “Active”, or “Enabled”, as they do not clearly communicate what the toggle controls.

overview.title: Accessibility
overview.section-content: A toggle must have a visible and persistent label and communicate its state programmatically as on or off. It should be operable via keyboard and include a visible focus indicator. The state should not rely on color alone to be understood, and the component must meet contrast requirements in all states.

dolist.text: - Use only for settings with two options

dontlist.text: Do not use toggles when the effect is delayed, unclear, or requires form submission to take effect
Do not rely only on color or position to communicate state
Avoid placing too many toggles close together, as it makes the interface harder to scan and increases the risk of mistakes

ux-text.title: Writing UX text for toggle
ux-text.section-content:
Labels should make the purpose obvious at a glance, show what the on and off state means, and avoid confusion or unnecessary wording. The goal is to help users feel confident about the choice they’re making. Write labels that describe the active state, not the action, be clear, and avoid vague wording or unclear terms, and don't use negatives as they can cause confusion.

**Do – labels that describe the active state:**

*Notifications* – On/Off

*Dark mode* – On/Off

**Don't – describe the action, be too vague or use negatives:**

*Turn on notification* or *Turn on dark mode –* On/Off

*Enable* – On/Off (too vague, enable what?)

Disable *–* On/Off

Don’t show *–* On/Off

ux-text.title: Keep labels short
ux-text.section-content:
One or two words is ideal.

**Do:** *Wi-Fi*, *Location*, *Auto-update*  
**Don’t:** *Automatically download updates in the background*

ux-text.title: Provide context where needed
ux-text.section-content:
Add a short description if the toggle affects something non-obvious.

**Example:**  
Auto-renew – On/Off  
*Your subscription will renew every month.*
